blocks.content: Davenport was almost at the door when he turned back.

It was a small thing — a half-turn, unhurried, the way a man turns when he just remembered something. But in a room full of people reading every movement for meaning, it landed like a sound in a quiet house.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: Mr. Mercer. One more thing. With Marcus gone — the debt arrangement with Price. Who handles that now? Is there any scenario in which his death financially benefits you?
The room went the kind of still that happens right before something breaks.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: It's a standard question in a financial—

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: No. No, you don't get to—
He was on his feet. The chair scraped back. His voice, which had been controlled all morning with the effort of a man holding something very heavy, came apart.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: He was my best friend. Twenty-three years. I have known that man since we were eighteen years old and shared a dorm room and had nothing and I am standing in a house where he is dead and you are asking me if his death benefits me—

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: I would have paid that debt a hundred times over to have him back in that room right now. You understand that? A hundred times. So don't you stand there and ask me if I—
His voice broke completely on the last word. He turned away. Put both hands on the windowsill and looked out at the beach and breathed.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: Mr. Mercer. I hear you. And I believe you.
He said it simply. No apology in it — it wasn't an apology — but something steadier. The tone of a man who asks hard questions and means no malice by them.
